days.completionMessage: Strong start, Dela. Every rep today built your foundation. Rest well — your body is already adapting.

good: Keep the pace easy — you should be able to hold a conversation
Focus on breathing deeply and letting the body warm up gradually
bad: Don't sprint or push hard — this is nervous system preparation, not cardio

good: Sit back into a chair just behind you
Feel your weight spread evenly through your whole foot
bad: Heels lifting or knees caving inward

good: Push the floor away from you as your hips rise
Squeeze your glutes hard at the very top
bad: Hyperextending the lower back — ribs stay down

good: Draw the biggest circle you can with your knee
Feel the hip joint rotating and opening
bad: Moving the torso instead of isolating the hip

good: Drive your heel into the box like you're stamping it
Feel your front glute pull you up — don't push off the back foot
bad: Using the back foot to push — all effort comes from the working leg only

good: Move like you're stepping over a low hurdle sideways
Stay light — feel the outer hip activate with each step
bad: Standing upright — keep a slight knee bend throughout

good: Hold the weight like a chalice close to your chest
Push your knees out over your small toes as you descend
bad: Elbows dropping and causing the torso to fall forward

good: Push your hips back toward the wall behind you
Feel the stretch building in the back of your legs as you lower
bad: Bending the knees too much — this is a hip hinge, not a squat

good: Press the platform away like you're pushing through the floor
Feel your lower back stay firmly pressed into the seat
bad: Knees caving inward on the push — actively drive them out

good: Curl your heels toward the seat in a smooth, controlled arc
Focus on the back of the thigh — feel it fully contract
bad: Hips lifting off the seat — pelvis stays pressed down throughout

days.completionMessage: Your upper body just got stronger, Dela. Eat well tonight — your muscles are rebuilding right now.

exercises.name: Rowing Machine or Arm Swings + March

good: If rowing, use long smooth strokes — legs drive first, then lean back, then pull
Feel your upper back and shoulders loosening up gradually
bad: Don't row with your arms alone — power comes from the legs and hips

good: Try to make the band twice as long by pulling it apart
Feel the muscles between your shoulder blades squeeze together
bad: Shrugging the shoulders — keep them down away from the ears

good: Keep arms, elbows, and backs of hands against the wall the whole time
Feel your shoulder blades glide down as your arms raise
bad: Lower back arching away from the wall

good: Round up to the ceiling, then dip your belly toward the floor
Feel each vertebra move one at a time — breathe with each rep
bad: Moving too fast — make it slow and intentional

good: Trace the biggest, smoothest circle you can with your arm
Feel the shoulder joint move through its full available range
bad: Compensating with the torso — keep the core still

good: Push the handles away like you're opening two heavy doors
Feel your chest muscles squeeze as arms extend
bad: Elbows flaring too wide — keep them at ~45° from the body

good: Pull the bar to your collarbone, elbows driving down to the floor
Feel the muscles under your armpits — your lats — doing the work
bad: Leaning too far back and turning it into a row

good: Press the dumbbells straight up toward the ceiling
Keep your core braced — feel your ribs stay down throughout
bad: Arching the lower back to help push the weight up

good: Pull the handle to your lower ribs — elbows travel past your sides
Feel your mid-back and shoulder blades squeeze at the end of each rep
bad: Using momentum — no rocking back and forward

good: Lead with your elbows — like you're slowly pouring a pitcher
Feel the outside of the shoulder — not the traps — working
bad: Swinging the dumbbells up with momentum

good: Keep elbows pinned to your sides like your arms are in a cast
Feel the back of your upper arm fully contract at the bottom
bad: Elbows drifting forward or using bodyweight to push down

good: Curl the weight up in a smooth arc — upper arms stay still
Squeeze the bicep hard at the top before lowering slowly
bad: Swinging the body — the movement comes only from the elbow

days.completionMessage: Three sessions down, Dela. You are building something real. Recover well — Day 4 closes the week strong.

good: Walk at a comfortable pace — arms swinging naturally at your sides
Use this time to breathe, arrive mentally, and prepare for the session
bad: Don't sit and rest instead — active warm-up is part of the work

good: Keep your lower back completely flat against the floor
Feel your deep core brace — like bracing for a light punch
bad: Lower back arching away from the floor as limbs extend

good: Reach your arm and opposite leg away from each other as far as possible
Feel your glute squeeze as the leg extends behind you
bad: Hips rotating — keep them level and square to the floor

good: Push hips up and hold — form a flat table from knees to shoulders
Squeeze glutes and brace core together at the top of each rep
bad: Feet placed too far forward — keep them directly under the knees

good: Step up and drive the opposite knee high like you're marching
Feel the front glute pull you up — not a push from the back leg
bad: Assisting with the back foot — all work from the working leg

good: Stay low and light — like you're ready to react to something
Feel the outer hip and glute engaging with every lateral step
bad: Standing upright — maintain an athletic quarter-squat position throughout

good: Push the floor away and stand tall — chest leads the way up
Feel your inner thighs and glutes working together to drive you up
bad: Rounding the upper or lower back — chest stays tall throughout

good: Step back and lower your back knee softly toward the floor
Feel the front leg — especially the glute — doing the lifting
bad: Front knee caving inward on the return — actively drive it out

good: Pull elbows back like you're trying to put them in your back pockets
Feel the mid-back squeeze — between the shoulder blades
bad: Rounding forward between reps — chest stays tall throughout

good: Rotate the palms outward as you press — like slowly opening a book
Feel the front and side of the shoulder working together
bad: Going too heavy and losing control of the rotation — start light

days.completionMessage: That's a full week done, Dela. Four sessions. This is who you are now. See you next week.

good: Pedal at a comfortable cadence — hips should stay level in the seat
Breathe steadily and feel the legs starting to loosen up
bad: Bouncing in the seat — set the seat height so legs are nearly fully extended

good: Draw the biggest circle possible with your knee
Feel the hip capsule opening with each rotation
bad: Swaying the torso — the movement is hip-only

good: Drive the single heel into the floor and push your hips to the ceiling
Feel the working glute doing all the lifting — no help from the back
bad: Hips dropping to one side — keep them level throughout

good: Open your top knee like a clamshell — heels stay together
Feel the outer glute (glute medius) working with each rep
bad: Rolling the pelvis backward to compensate — keep hips stacked

good: From standing, lower into a deep squat, pause, then slowly stand tall
Feel your hips open and your spine decompress with each rep
bad: Rushing through it — this is slow and deliberate joint preparation

good: Slight bounce with a knee drive — like a slow, exaggerated skip
Stay light and find the rhythm — feel the ground contact
bad: Heavy landings — aim for quiet, soft foot contacts each time

good: Drive your hips to the ceiling — form a straight line hip to knee
Squeeze your glutes hard at the top and hold for a full count
bad: Overextending the lower back at the top — ribs stay down

good: Kick your heel toward the wall behind you in a controlled arc
Feel the glute fully contract at the end of the movement
bad: Swinging the leg — slow and controlled through the full range

good: Feet wide, toes out — sit between your legs, not over them
Feel inner thighs and glutes working together on the way up
bad: Knees collapsing inward — actively drive them out on every rep

good: Curl your heels toward your glutes in a slow, smooth arc
Feel the hamstrings fully contract — hold briefly at the top
bad: Hips lifting off the pad — keep the pelvis firmly pressed down

cards.title: Sleep & Recovery
cards.body: Your sleep score is currently 4/10 — this is your biggest recovery lever right now. Prioritize a consistent bedtime, reduce screen light 30 minutes before bed, and keep your room cool and dark. Better sleep directly equals better results from every session you do. Recovery happens while you sleep, not while you train.

cards.title: Managing Your Stress Load
cards.body: With a stress level of 9/10, your body is already under significant demand before you touch a weight. Keep sessions to 60 minutes maximum, never train to failure this month, and treat rest days as part of the program — not a break from it. Training smart under high stress is a real skill. You are learning it now.

cards.title: Nutrition Basics
cards.body: To build muscle and lose fat simultaneously, prioritize protein at every meal. Aim for 1.6–2.0g per kg of bodyweight daily — at 72kg that means roughly 115–145g of protein per day. Don't fear carbohydrates around training — they fuel your sessions. Focus on whole foods, consistent meal timing, and staying well hydrated throughout the day.

cards.title: Understanding RPE
cards.body: RPE stands for Rate of Perceived Exertion, on a scale of 1–10. RPE 6 means the weight is moderately challenging but you could do 4 or more additional reps. RPE 7 means you're working hard with about 3 reps left in the tank. This month stays RPE 6–7. You are not training to failure. Technique always comes before intensity.

cards.title: Progressive Overload
cards.body: Each week, try to make one small improvement: add 1–2 reps to a set, improve your tempo control, or add a small amount of weight to an exercise. You don't need to do all three — just one improvement per session is enough to drive consistent progress over months. Use the weight log field on each exercise to track what you lifted.

cards.title: Weekly Check-In
cards.body: At the end of each training week, take 2 minutes to reflect: (1) Which exercises felt strongest? (2) Where did you struggle with form? (3) How was your energy across sessions? Share this with your coach. These notes are what shape your next program and keep your results moving in the right direction.
